county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1838; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Caldwell Parish Clerk of Court (Columbia). Formed 1838 from Catahoula and Ouachita. North-central Louisiana uplands. Pre-1838 records split between Catahoula and Ouachita. Small, rural parish with predominantly Anglo-American settlement. Cotton farming and timber heritage. Caldwell Parish Library has limited local history materials. Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist) are important sources. Cross-reference with Catahoula and Ouachita for earlier family records. Columbia was a river town on the Ouachita River. Check BLM land patents for original land entries.

source_profiles.repository: Caldwell Parish Clerk of Court, Columbia
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Conveyance (deed) books for Caldwell Parish from 1838 forward; Louisiana uses civil law system — property transfers recorded as notarial acts

source_profiles.source_name: Caldwell Parish Succession Records — Clerk of Court
source_profiles.repository: Caldwell Parish Clerk of Court, Columbia
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Succession records (Louisiana's civil law equivalent of probate) for Caldwell Parish; includes inventories, family meetings (conseil de famille), and partition records naming heirs

source_profiles.repository: Caldwell Parish Clerk of Court / Louisiana State Archives / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil marriage licenses and Catholic church marriage registers for Caldwell Parish; church records often predate civil registration
source_profiles.survival_notes: Statewide vital records registration from 1914; earlier records at parish level and in church registers

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane Audrey (1957) destroyed courthouse and many records; Hurricane Rita (2005) caused additional damage
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1870; Reconstruction-era formation; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Cameron Parish Clerk of Court (Cameron). Formed 1870 from Calcasieu and Vermilion. BURNED PARISH — Hurricane Audrey (1957) destroyed the courthouse and many records; Hurricane Rita (2005) caused further destruction. Louisiana's largest parish by area but smallest by population. Cheniere (Chenier) region — Cajun fishing and trapping communities. Cameron Parish Library has rebuilt some local history collections. Pre-1870 records in Calcasieu and Vermilion. Catholic church registers at Our Lady Star of the Sea (Cameron). Check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies. Menhaden fishing industry records.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1851 courthouse fire destroyed early records; 1882 fire destroyed additional records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1808; formed during territorial period (pre-statehood); Central Louisiana
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Catahoula Parish Clerk of Court (Harrisonburg). Formed 1808 from Rapides. BURNED PARISH — 1851 and 1882 courthouse fires destroyed early records. Named for Catahoula Lake and the Catahoula people. Pre-1808 records in Rapides. Check Louisiana State Archives for surviving state copies and colonial records. French colonial presence along the Ouachita and Black Rivers. Jonesville and Harrisonburg area families. Mixed French Creole and Anglo-American heritage. Parent parish for LaSalle (1908). Church records help fill gaps from courthouse fires. Sicily Island area settlement records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1828; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Claiborne Parish Clerk of Court (Homer). Formed 1828 from Natchitoches. Named for Governor William C.C. Claiborne. North Louisiana uplands — cotton farming area with significant enslaved population. Pre-1828 records in Natchitoches. Homer is the parish seat. Claiborne Parish Library has local genealogical materials. Parent parish for Bienville (1848), Bossier (1843), and parts of Lincoln, Union, and Webster. Protestant church records (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian) predominate. Germantown Colony records (1835 utopian community). Cross-reference with Natchitoches for earlier records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Civil War destruction (1863) and Mississippi River flooding destroyed some early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1807; original Orleans Territory parish; Central Louisiana
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Concordia Parish Clerk of Court (Vidalia). Original Orleans Territory parish (1807). BURNED PARISH — Civil War destruction and Mississippi River flooding caused record losses. Directly across the Mississippi from Natchez, MS — families frequently appear in both Concordia and Adams County, MS records. Major cotton plantation parish with extensive enslaved persons records in successions. Vidalia and Ferriday area. Concordia Parish Library has local history. Cross-reference with Natchez records extensively. Parent parish for Tensas (1843) and Madison (1838). Spanish colonial records at Louisiana State Archives.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1843; antebellum parish; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and De Soto Parish Clerk of Court (Mansfield). Formed 1843 from Natchitoches and Caddo. Site of the Battle of Mansfield (1864) — last major Confederate victory. Pre-1843 records in Natchitoches and Caddo. Cotton plantation parish with significant enslaved population. Mansfield Female College records. De Soto Parish Library has local history materials. Red River area families. Cross-reference with Natchitoches and Caddo. Battle of Pleasant Hill (1864) also in parish area. Mansfield State Historic Site has Civil War records and research resources.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1810; formed during territorial period (pre-statehood); River Road plantation region
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives (located in this parish) and East Baton Rouge Parish Clerk of Court (Baton Rouge). Formed 1810 from Spanish West Florida territory. Baton Rouge is the state capital. LSU (Louisiana State University) Hill Memorial Library Special Collections has extensive Louisiana manuscript collections including plantation records, family papers, and the T. Harry Williams Center for Oral History. The Louisiana State Archives is the primary state repository for all parish records. East Baton Rouge Parish Library has a genealogy department. Multi-ethnic heritage — French, Spanish, British (1763-1779), Creole, and African American. Catholic and Protestant church records both important.

county_metadata.parent_county: Carroll (divided into East and West Carroll)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1877; Reconstruction-era formation; North Louisiana uplands
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and East Carroll Parish Clerk of Court (Lake Providence). Formed 1877 when Carroll Parish was divided into East and West Carroll. Pre-1877 records in Carroll Parish (check both East and West clerks). Mississippi River delta cotton plantation country with large enslaved population. Lake Providence area. One of the most impoverished parishes in Louisiana. Cross-reference with Madison and West Carroll for related families. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA/FamilySearch document post-Civil War transitions. East Carroll Parish Library has limited local history resources.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1824; antebellum parish; River Road plantation region
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and East Feliciana Parish Clerk of Court (Clinton). Formed 1824 when Feliciana Parish was divided into East and West. Part of the Florida Parishes — under Spanish and then briefly independent Republic of West Florida (1810) before US annexation. Clinton is a beautifully preserved antebellum town. Significant cotton plantation parish with large enslaved population. Pre-1824 records in Feliciana Parish. The Lawyers' Row in Clinton has historic courthouse. East Feliciana Parish Library has local history. Audubon State Historic Site (Oakley Plantation). Cross-reference with West Feliciana for related families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Louisiana parish formed 1910; 20th-century formation; Acadian/Cajun heartland
county_metadata.research_tips: Louisiana State Archives and Evangeline Parish Clerk of Court (Ville Platte). Formed 1910 from St. Landry — relatively young parish. Named for Longfellow's poem about the Acadian exile. Heart of Cajun prairie culture. Pre-1910 records in St. Landry. Ville Platte is known as the Smoked Meat Capital of the World. Catholic church registers essential — Sacred Heart of Jesus (Ville Platte). French-speaking Acadian families predominate. Evangeline Parish Library has local history collections. Le Vieux Village (Acadian heritage museum). Cross-reference with St. Landry for earlier records. Cajun music and Zydeco cultural heritage.
